comment_text.sn11.17:1.3: The form of this discourse echoes that of SN 11.8, with Sakka and Sahampati in the roles of Verocana and Sakka. This time it is Sakka who gives the inferior verse that is trumped by Sahampati.
comment_text.sn11.17:2.4: Sakka speaks to the personal liberation of the Buddha.
comment_text.sn11.17:4.4: Sahampati encourages the Buddha to teach for the liberation of all beings, as he did after the Buddha’s awakening (SN 6.1:8.1).

root_text.sn11.17:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena bhagavā divāvihāragato hoti paṭisallīno.
root_text.sn11.17:1.3: Atha kho sakko ca devānamindo brahmā ca sahampati yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā paccekaṁ dvārabāhaṁ nissāya aṭṭhaṁsu.
root_text.sn11.17:1.4: Atha kho sakko devānamindo bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:

translation_text.sn11.17:1.2: Now at that time the Buddha had gone into retreat for the day’s meditation.
translation_text.sn11.17:1.3: Then Sakka, lord of gods, and the divinity Sahampati approached the Buddha and stationed themselves one by each door-post.
translation_text.sn11.17:1.4: Then Sakka spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence:
translation_text.sn11.17:2.1: “Rise, hero! Victor in battle, with burden put down,
translation_text.sn11.17:2.2: wander the world free of debt.
translation_text.sn11.17:2.4: like the moon on the fifteenth night.”
translation_text.sn11.17:3.1: “Lord of gods, that’s not how to pay homage to the Realized Ones.
translation_text.sn11.17:3.2: This is how it should be done:
translation_text.sn11.17:4.1: ‘Rise, hero! Victor in battle, caravan leader,
translation_text.sn11.17:4.2: wander the world free of debt.
translation_text.sn11.17:4.3: Let the Blessed One teach the Dhamma!
translation_text.sn11.17:4.4: There will be those who understand!’”
